title: About
text: Hello my name is Zoe! Here is my blog, which consists of interesting data about sports and some of my interests. I am a junior here at the University of Nebraska at Lincoln studying sports media and broadcasting.

title: The Story of Half-Court Shots in College Basketball
text:
Half-court shots are some of the most dramatic and memorable moments in college basketball, often happening in desperate, last-second situations.
Before looking at when half-court shots occur or whether they are successful, it is important to first understand just how rare they are. Instead of looking at all shots, I focused on the longest attempts in the dataset and compared how often different teams attempt them. This helps show whether half-court shots are a regular part of any team’s offense or simply rare, last-second situations.


Code
library(tidyverse)


── Attaching core tidyverse packages ──────────────────────── tidyverse 2.0.0 ──
✔ dplyr     1.1.4     ✔ readr     2.1.6
✔ forcats   1.0.1     ✔ stringr   1.6.0
✔ ggplot2   4.0.1     ✔ tibble    3.3.1
✔ lubridate 1.9.4     ✔ tidyr     1.3.2
✔ purrr     1.2.1     
── Conflicts ────────────────────────────────────────── tidyverse_conflicts() ──
✖ dplyr::filter() masks stats::filter()
✖ dplyr::lag()    masks stats::lag()
ℹ Use the conflicted package (<http://conflicted.r-lib.org/>) to force all conflicts to become errors






Code
library(hoopR)




Code
library(dplyr)
library(ggplot2)




Code
library(hoopR)
library(dplyr)
library(ggplot2)

pbp_data <- load_mbb_pbp(seasons = 2025)




Code
half_court_team <- pbp_data %>%
  filter(points_attempted > 0) %>%   # REAL SHOTS ONLY
  filter(!is.na(coordinate_x) & !is.na(coordinate_y)) %>%
  mutate(
    shot_distance = sqrt(coordinate_x^2 + coordinate_y^2),
    team = ifelse(team_id == home_team_id, home_team_name, away_team_name)
  ) %>%
  filter(shot_distance >= quantile(shot_distance, 0.9998, na.rm = TRUE))




Code
team_counts <- half_court_team %>%
  group_by(team) %>%
  summarise(
    attempts = n()
  ) %>%
  arrange(desc(attempts)) %>%
  slice_head(n = 10)




Code
ggplot(team_counts, aes(x = reorder(team, attempts), y = attempts)) +
  geom_col(fill = "steelblue") +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    title = "Half-Court Shots are a Last Resort",
    x = "Team",
    y = "Half-Court Shot Attempts"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 16, face = "bold")
  )









The difference is clear: while teams take dozens of normal shots in a single game, half-court attempts occur at a rate close to zero. This shows that half-court shots are not just uncommon, but extremely rare events. They are not part of regular offensive strategy, but instead represent rare, last-second attempts when players have no other option.
Half-court shots may be rare, but they are also not random. Instead, they tend to occur in very specific moments within a game. To explore this, I examined when long-distance shots are taken, focusing on the final seconds of each half.




Code
half_court_time <- pbp_data %>%
  filter(shooting_play == 1) %>%
  filter(!is.na(coordinate_x) & !is.na(coordinate_y)) %>%
  mutate(
    shot_distance = sqrt(coordinate_x^2 + coordinate_y^2),
    seconds_remaining = clock_minutes * 60 + clock_seconds
  ) %>%
  filter(shot_distance >= 35)   




Code
half_court_time <- half_court_time %>%
  filter(seconds_remaining <= 30)




Code
ggplot(half_court_time, aes(x = seconds_remaining)) +
  geom_bar() +
  labs(
    title = "Long-Distance Shots Happen at the End of Halves",
    x = "Seconds Remaining in Half",
    y = "Number of Shots"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 16, face = "bold")
  )









The results show a clear pattern: long-distance shots overwhelmingly occur in the final seconds of a half. Rather than being part of normal offensive strategy, these attempts are typically last-second efforts when players have no better option before time expires.
While extreme long-distance shots are rare, some do go in. This raises an interesting question: are certain teams more successful than others at making these shots, or are they simply random occurrences?

Because the hoopR coordinate system does not directly correspond to exact court distances, I define half-court-style shots as the longest attempts in the dataset.


Code
team_makes <- pbp_data %>%
  filter(points_attempted > 0) %>%
  filter(!is.na(coordinate_x) & !is.na(coordinate_y)) %>%
  mutate(
    shot_distance = sqrt(coordinate_x^2 + coordinate_y^2),
    result = ifelse(scoring_play == TRUE, 1, 0),
    team = ifelse(team_id == home_team_id, home_team_name, away_team_name)
  ) %>%
  filter(result == 1) %>%   
  filter(shot_distance >= quantile(shot_distance, 0.999, na.rm = TRUE)) %>%
  group_by(team) %>%
  summarise(makes = n()) %>%
  arrange(desc(makes)) %>%
  slice_head(n = 10)




Code
ggplot(team_makes, aes(x = reorder(team, makes), y = makes)) +
  geom_col(fill = "darkred") +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    title = "Teams With the Most Made Half-Court-Style Shots",
    x = "Team",
    y = "Made Shots"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 16, face = "bold")
  )









Even the teams with the most makes only have a few successful attempts. This shows that these shots are not something teams can rely on, but instead are rare and unpredictable plays.
Extreme long-distance shots show up in highlights and feel like big moments, but the numbers tell a different story. Teams don’t take them often, and when they do, it’s almost always at the end of a half when there aren’t many other options. Even the teams that have made the most of these shots only have a few, which makes it clear there’s no real skill or consistency behind them.
So what looks like a special play is really just a low-percentage attempt that occasionally goes in. Most of the time, it doesn’t.
